politician.name: Raphael Gamaliel Warnock
politician.professional_background: Senior pastor of Ebenezer Baptist Church (Atlanta) since 2005; civil-rights organizer
politician.profile_narrative: Raphael Warnock represents Georgia in the U.S. Senate since January 2021, winning the runoff alongside Jon Ossoff that gave Democrats Senate control. He won re-election in November 2022 over Herschel Walker after a runoff. He is senior pastor of Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta (the church where Martin Luther King Jr. preached). His Senate priorities include voting rights, healthcare access, agricultural policy for Georgia, and infrastructure investment.

promises.promise_text: Cap insulin costs for Medicare beneficiaries.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Warnock co-led the $35/month Medicare insulin cap that was included in the Inflation Reduction Act (August 2022). Took effect January 2023.

promises.promise_text: Restore voting rights / pass John Lewis VRA.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored John Lewis VRA Advancement Act; cloture failed multiple times (Republican filibuster + Manchin/Sinema rule-change opposition).
IRA Medicare drug-pricing negotiation passed (Warnock YES). 10 drugs initially, expanding to 60 by 2029.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed 49-51 (Manchin NO). Roe overturned by Dobbs (2022); federal codification did not occur.
Voted YES on IRA ($369B climate).
BSCA passed (Warnock YES). Broader assault-weapons ban did not pass.
Co-sponsored Justice for Black Farmers Act framework. Secured USDA equity provisions in IRA. Georgia-specific Farm Bill priorities advanced.
Supported Biden's broad cancellation (SCOTUS-struck). ~$138B narrower cancellations approved.
Voted YES on cloture; failed Republican filibuster. RFMA passed (Warnock YES).
Voted YES on Justice Jackson + most lower-court Biden nominees.
Voted YES on IIJA (2021).
Voted YES on Ukraine aid packages.
Campaigned for Harris-Walz; Trump won. Substantive outcome not achieved.
Won December 2022 runoff against Herschel Walker 51-49. Whether this counts in the scoring framework is contested.

politician.name: Cory Anthony Booker
politician.professional_background: Former Mayor of Newark, NJ (2006-2013); Yale Law graduate; Rhodes Scholar
politician.profile_narrative: Cory Booker represents New Jersey in the U.S. Senate since October 2013. He ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2020 (withdrew January 2020). He serves on Judiciary, Foreign Relations, and Small Business Committees. His priorities include criminal justice reform, racial equity, agricultural policy, and healthcare access.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-author of First Step Act (December 2018, signed P.L. 115-391) — most significant federal criminal-justice reform in a generation. Reduced mandatory minimums for some drug offenses; expanded compassionate-release access.
Co-sponsored multiple reparations-study and racial-equity measures. Baby Bonds framework introduced; did not advance.

promises.promise_text: Pass George Floyd Justice in Policing Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Booker was lead Senate negotiator on bipartisan policing reform 2020-2021. Negotiations collapsed September 2021 after Booker and Sen. Tim Scott (R-SC) could not bridge qualified-immunity provisions. No federal policing reform enacted.
Voted YES on IRA. Co-sponsored multiple climate framework bills.
Cloture failed on Freedom to Vote Act and JLVRA.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed.
IRA drug-price negotiation passed (Booker YES).
BSCA passed (Booker YES).
Cloture failed; RFMA passed.
IRA's 15% corporate minimum tax + 1% buyback tax (Booker YES).
Voted YES on Justice Jackson + lower-court Biden nominees.
Voted YES on Israel aid.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
Withdrew January 2020 before Iowa caucus. Did not achieve nomination.

politician.name: Amy Jean Klobuchar
politician.professional_background: Former Hennepin County Attorney (MN); 2020 Democratic presidential primary candidate
politician.profile_narrative: Amy Klobuchar represents Minnesota in the U.S. Senate since January 2007. She ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2020 (withdrew March 2020 to endorse Biden). She chairs the Senate Rules Committee. Her priorities include antitrust enforcement, prescription drug pricing, agricultural policy, and election security.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored American Innovation and Choice Online Act + Open App Markets Act with Sen. Grassley. Voted YES on Khan FTC confirmation. Khan-era FTC pursued major antitrust cases.
IRA Medicare drug-pricing negotiation (Klobuchar YES).
Co-led Electoral Count Reform Act (Klobuchar YES, Dec 2022, 88-11).
As Senate Rules chair, led Freedom to Vote Act; cloture failed.
Voted YES on IRA.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed.
IIJA (Klobuchar YES).
BSCA passed (Klobuchar YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most lower-court nominees.
Multiple Farm Bill priorities advanced. Rural broadband via IIJA.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
Cloture failed. RFMA passed (Klobuchar YES).
Withdrew March 2020 to endorse Biden.
Inflation peaked at 9.1% (June 2022), declined to ~3% by 2024. Klobuchar voted for both ARPA (inflation-driver per critics) and IRA (anti-inflation per supporters).

politician.name: Sherrod Campbell Brown
politician.current_status: Former senator — lost November 2024 re-election to Bernie Moreno (R)
politician.professional_background: Former Ohio Secretary of State; U.S. Representative OH-13 (1993-2007); long-time pro-labor advocate
politician.profile_narrative: Sherrod Brown represented Ohio in the U.S. Senate from January 2007 to January 2025. He lost his November 2024 re-election to Republican Bernie Moreno by approximately 4 percentage points in Ohio's continued Republican-trending federal environment. He chaired the Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee 2021-2025. His career framing was economic-populist Democrat — pro-labor, pro-manufacturing, skeptical of finance and trade liberalization.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted NO on S. 2155 (2018 Dodd-Frank rollback). As Banking chair (2021-2025), conducted oversight on SVB/Signature failures, FTX, and pharmacy benefit managers. Major Wall Street reform legislation did not pass.

promises.promise_text: Bring back manufacturing jobs / fair trade.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Voted YES on CHIPS Act ($280B for semiconductor manufacturing). Co-sponsored Reciprocal Trade Act. IRA included Buy American provisions Brown supported.
PRO Act failed Senate cloture. Brown co-sponsored. Outcome not achieved.
Lost November 2024 to Bernie Moreno by ~4 points in Ohio's continued Republican-trending federal environment.
No cuts during tenure.
IRA Medicare negotiation (Brown YES).
Voted YES on IRA. Ohio coal/manufacturing-state framing required moderation on more aggressive climate measures.
Cloture failed.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed.
IIJA (Brown YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most.
BSCA passed (Brown YES).
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
Co-sponsored Railway Safety Act after February 2023 East Palestine derailment. Bill did not advance to floor vote. Norfolk Southern paid major damages settlement.
Cloture failed. RFMA passed (Brown YES).

politician.name: Kirsten Elizabeth Gillibrand
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative NY-20 (2007-2009); appointed to Senate seat 2009 after Hillary Clinton's appointment as Secretary of State
politician.profile_narrative: Kirsten Gillibrand represents New York in the U.S. Senate since January 2009. She ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2020 (withdrew August 2019). She chairs the Senate Armed Services Subcommittee on Personnel. Her priorities include military justice reform (notably removing sexual-assault prosecutions from chain of command), reproductive rights, paid family leave, and 9/11 first-responder healthcare.

promises.promise_text: Reform military justice / remove sexual assault from chain of command.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Gillibrand's decade-long campaign culminated in the FY2022 NDAA which moved prosecution of sexual assault, harassment, and related offenses out of the commander's chain of command to independent military prosecutors. Signed December 2021.
Co-led permanent extension of the Zadroga Act + September 11th Victim Compensation Fund (signed July 2019). Permanently funded through 2090.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed (Manchin NO). Federal codification did not occur.
FAMILY Act introduced multiple Congresses; never advanced. BBB paid-leave provisions died with BBB. IRA did not include paid leave.
Voted YES on IRA.
Cloture failed.
IRA negotiation (Gillibrand YES).
BSCA passed (Gillibrand YES). Assault weapons ban did not.
IIJA (Gillibrand YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
Cloture failed; RFMA passed.
Multiple oversight hearings on defense contracting. Substantive reform legislation limited.
Withdrew August 2019.

politician.name: Richard Blumenthal
politician.profile_narrative: Richard Blumenthal represents Connecticut in the U.S. Senate since January 2011. He chairs the Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations and the Senate Veterans' Affairs Committee. His priorities include consumer protection, veterans' affairs, gun safety, and antitrust enforcement.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-led PACT Act with Sen. Tester. Signed August 10, 2022.
Co-led BSCA negotiations. Connecticut Sandy Hook context drives his prominence on this issue.
Multiple Section 230 reform bills introduced; KIDS Act on online child safety advanced through committee but did not pass floor.
IRA Medicare negotiation (Blumenthal YES).

Supported Khan FTC. Co-sponsored AICOA + Open App Markets.
IIJA (Blumenthal YES).

Multiple consumer-protection bills introduced; CFPB preserved.
Multiple PSI hearings on vaping. FDA enforcement increased; teen-vaping rates declined modestly. Whether 'investigation' translated to outcomes is contested.

politician.name: Christopher Scott Murphy
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative CT-5 (2007-2013); former Connecticut state legislator
politician.profile_narrative: Chris Murphy represents Connecticut in the U.S. Senate since January 2013. He won re-election in November 2024 by approximately 17 percentage points. He is the lead Senate Democrat on gun safety (Sandy Hook in his House district shaped his career focus). He led the 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill negotiations with Sen. Lankford (R-OK).

promises.verdict_reasoning: Murphy led Senate Democratic negotiations on BSCA after Uvalde shooting (May 2022). BSCA signed June 25, 2022 — first major federal gun-safety legislation in 30 years.
Murphy was lead Senate Democratic negotiator on the February 2024 Bipartisan Border Bill (Lankford-Murphy-Sinema). Bill collapsed after Trump opposition triggered House Republican rejection.

promises.promise_text: Reform Saudi arms sales / Yemen war policy.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Co-sponsored multiple Saudi arms-sale block resolutions. Some passed; Trump vetoed during his first term. Biden ended offensive support for Saudi-led Yemen operations (February 2021). Yemen ceasefire (2022).
Voted YES on IRA drug-pricing. Connecticut state-level public-option exploration ongoing.
Voted YES on IRA.
Cloture failed.
Voted YES on WHPA cloture; failed.
IIJA (Murphy YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
BSCA included $11B+ for mental-health and school-security funding. Broader mental-health reform did not pass.
Cloture failed; RFMA passed.
Won November 2024 re-election 59-39 over Matthew Corey.
Supported sanctions packages. As Foreign Relations subcommittee member, led Russia oversight.
Murphy has positioned himself for future Democratic Party leadership roles. Substantive trajectory uncertain.

politician.name: Patricia Lynn Murray
politician.current_status: Serving sixth Senate term; Senate President pro tempore (2023+)
politician.professional_background: Former Washington state legislator; longest-serving female senator in U.S. history
politician.profile_narrative: Patty Murray represents Washington in the U.S. Senate since January 1993, serving her sixth term — the longest-serving female senator in U.S. history. She has held Senate President pro tempore since January 2023 (third-in-line for presidential succession). She chairs the Senate Appropriations Committee. She is among the most senior Senate Democrats and has been instrumental in appropriations negotiations across Republican and Democratic administrations.

promises.verdict_reasoning: Murray co-led WHPA negotiations as senior Democrat. Cloture failed (Manchin NO). Federal codification did not occur but she pursued every available path.
As Appropriations chair (2023+), led FY2024 funding negotiations. Multiple CRs and the March 2024 minibus passed under her leadership.
Murray led Senate-side BBB childcare/pre-K provisions. BBB failed; IRA did not include them.
Voted YES on IRA.
Cloture failed.
IRA negotiation (Murray YES).
IIJA (Murray YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most.
BSCA passed (Murray YES).
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
PREVENT Pandemics Act enacted (2022). ARPA pandemic response. Broader reform did not advance.
Cloture failed; RFMA passed.
FAA Reauthorization Act of 2024 included strengthened aviation-safety oversight provisions Murray supported.
Voted YES on Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson. Voted NO on Barrett (citing timing, not qualifications).

politician.name: Richard Joseph Durbin
politician.current_status: Serving fifth Senate term; Senate Majority Whip (was 2021-2025)
politician.professional_background: Former U.S. Representative IL-20 (1983-1997); chair of Senate Judiciary Committee 2021-2025
politician.profile_narrative: Dick Durbin represents Illinois in the U.S. Senate since January 1997, serving his fifth term. He served as Senate Majority Whip (second-ranking Democrat) under Schumer's leadership 2021-2025. He chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee 2021-2025, overseeing the confirmation of Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson and hundreds of lower-court judges.

promises.verdict_reasoning: As Judiciary chair 2021-2025, oversaw confirmation of Justice Jackson + approximately 235 lower-court judges.
First Step Act (2018, Durbin YES). EQUAL Act (eliminating crack/powder sentencing disparity) passed House but failed Senate.

DREAM Act introduced by Durbin in multiple Congresses since 2001 — never passed. DACA program continues under executive action; courts have repeatedly upheld but the program lacks statutory grounding.
Voted YES on IRA.
IRA negotiation (Durbin YES).
WHPA cloture failed.

Voted YES on Justice Jackson. Voted NO on Trump-era SCOTUS picks citing process concerns.
As Judiciary chair, held SCOTUS ethics hearings on Thomas/Alito gifts and disclosures. Supreme Court Ethics Reform Act introduced; did not advance.

promises.promise_text: Negotiate end-of-career legacy / mentor next leadership.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Durbin remains in Senate as elder statesman. Has not announced retirement. Whether his leadership transition has been adequate is contested.

politician.name: Timothy Michael Kaine
politician.professional_background: Former Governor of Virginia (2006-2010); Mayor of Richmond, VA (1998-2001); Hillary Clinton's 2016 running mate
politician.profile_narrative: Tim Kaine represents Virginia in the U.S. Senate since January 2013. He was Hillary Clinton's 2016 vice-presidential running mate. He won re-election in November 2024 by approximately 9 percentage points. He serves on Armed Services, Foreign Relations, HELP, and Budget Committees. His priorities include war-powers reform, healthcare access, and military family support.

promises.promise_text: Reform war-powers / require congressional authorization for military action.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Kaine co-led repeal of 1991 and 2002 Iraq AUMFs (Senate passed March 2023; House did not act). Iran War Powers Resolution (February 2020, Kaine YES) passed Senate, vetoed by Trump.
Co-sponsored multiple military-family support provisions in NDAAs. Helped secure Virginia base reinvestments.
IRA expanded ACA subsidies (Kaine YES). Public option did not pass.
WHPA cloture failed. Kaine voted YES; outcome not achieved.
Voted YES on IRA.
Cloture failed.
IRA negotiation (Kaine YES).
IIJA (Kaine YES).
Voted YES on Jackson + most.
Voted YES on Ukraine aid.
BSCA passed (Kaine YES).
Cloture failed; RFMA passed.

Clinton-Kaine lost 2016 to Trump-Pence in Electoral College (Clinton won popular vote).
